report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Tara, thank you for taking the time to share this with us. Your answers paint a really clear picture, and we want you to know that what you're experiencing has real, biological explanations. Let's walk through what stands out.

### Your Body Is Running on Stress Hormones

This is the most striking pattern in your answers, and it ties almost everything together. You fast most of the day, have some coffee, and then eat a big dinner. Here's what that means biologically: when your body goes without food for extended periods, blood sugar drops, and your brain perceives that as a threat. To keep you alive and functioning, it ramps up stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline to mobilize stored energy. Coffee amplifies this further.

So for most of the day, you're essentially running on a stress response rather than on fuel from food. This is likely a major reason you feel "on edge" and hypervigilant. That jitteriness and restlessness you described aren't character flaws or a psychological issue. They're the physical sensation of elevated adrenaline doing its job to keep your blood sugar from crashing. The fact that you identified yourself as blood sugar sensitive fits perfectly here.

### Why Eating Makes You More Anxious, Not Less

You mentioned that your anxiety worsens after eating, which can feel confusing. If stress hormones have been propping you up all day, when food finally arrives and blood sugar stabilizes, those hormones start to drop. This transition can paradoxically feel destabilizing. On top of that, when a large meal hits a digestive system that has been essentially dormant all day, it can trigger its own cascade of gut-level stress signals. The nausea you experience is a strong clue that your gut is reacting poorly to this pattern, possibly involving elevated serotonin in the digestive tract, which is produced in response to gut irritation and directly promotes nausea, anxiety, and that "wired but unwell" feeling.

### A Gut-Brain Connection Worth Paying Attention To

Most people don't realize that roughly 90% of the body's serotonin is produced in the gut, not the brain. When the gut is irritated or under stress, serotonin production ramps up, and this gut-derived serotonin stimulates the stress axis, increases cortisol, and can drive the exact hypervigilant, on-edge state you're describing. Your nausea is one of the hallmark signs of excess gut serotonin. The fact that you're currently on an SSRI is worth noting here as well. We would never suggest changing your medication, but understanding that your body may already be producing significant serotonin from the gut adds an important layer to the full picture that deserves exploration.

### Your Sleep Is Telling the Same Story

Waking during the night with anxiety is one of the clearest signs that stress hormones are spiking when they shouldn't be. During sleep, your body relies on stored energy (primarily from the liver) to maintain blood sugar through the night. When those stores are insufficient, which is far more likely when you've only eaten one meal, your body activates adrenaline and cortisol to compensate. You wake up anxious not because of your thoughts, but because your biology just hit an alarm button. This disrupted sleep then compounds everything the next day: lower resilience, more reliance on coffee and stress hormones, and the cycle deepens.

### Signs Your Metabolism Needs Support

You reported frequently cold extremities, which is one of the most reliable indicators of a sluggish thyroid and a lower metabolic rate. Your energy level of 6 out of 10 aligns with this as well. Thyroid hormones are the master regulators of metabolism. When they're low, everything slows: digestion becomes sluggish (contributing to nausea and gut issues), the body produces less heat (cold hands, feet, and nose), mood-regulating neurosteroids decline, and the stress response becomes more easily triggered. A system that's already underfueled and then asked to fast most of the day is under enormous pressure.

### The Bigger Picture

Tara, your symptoms are not random and they are not "just anxiety." What we see is a system that is energy-deprived, running on stress chemistry instead of real fuel, with a gut that is signaling distress and a metabolism that is asking for more support. The hypervigilance, the nausea, the broken sleep, the cold extremities, and the post-meal anxiety all connect back to the same underlying patterns. Your goal of not feeling so on edge is absolutely within reach when these root causes are addressed rather than managed around.

---

A quiz can identify patterns, but your situation has layers that deserve a real conversation. If you'd like to explore what's driving these patterns in more depth and what a path forward could look like for you specifically, we offer a completely free consultation with a member of our team. No obligation, no pressure. Just a chance to talk through your unique picture with someone who understands this approach. You can book using the button below whenever you're ready.
